# Figure description: скв_155.jpg

**Source**: `36572_Smolianova_1984/raw_graphics/Колонки_ скважин/скв_155.jpg`  
**Generated by**: claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## Figure Type

This is a **composite well log (borehole log / каротажная диаграмма)** — a standard geophysical well log strip chart typical of Soviet-era mineral exploration drilling. It appears to be a multi-track wireline log presentation combining several geophysical logging curves recorded continuously with depth, accompanied by a lithological/stratigraphic column and tabular data sections. This is the type of document known in Russian practice as a **"геолого-геофизический разрез скважины"** (geological-geophysical borehole cross-section) or composite log sheet.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The figure covers a **single deep borehole** drilled as part of the regional survey described in Report 36572. Given the regional context (map sheets M-42 and N-42, northern Kazakhstan), this is likely one of the parametric or structural exploration wells drilled to evaluate subsurface geology. The log appears to cover a substantial depth interval — based on the length of the strip and the scale typical of Soviet 1:200,000 surveys, likely spanning several hundred to potentially **1,000–2,000+ metres** of depth. The borehole number (скважина №) and map sheet designation would be in the header at the top of the document, though the image resolution makes precise transcription difficult.

---

## Key Content

The composite log is organized into several vertical tracks running the full depth of the borehole:

**Left section:**
- A **lithological column** (литологическая колонка) showing rock types encountered at depth, rendered with standard Soviet lithological symbols (patterns indicating sandstone, clay/shale, limestone, crystalline basement, etc.)
- Stratigraphic formation labels appear along the left margin, likely indicating system/stage assignments (e.g., Paleozoic basement, Mesozoic cover sequences)
- Depth markings in metres appear at regular intervals

**Central tracks (geophysical curves):**
- **Two prominent wiggle-trace curves** run the full depth of the log, representing geophysical measurements. Based on Soviet logging practice, these are most likely:
  - **KS (КС) — Electrical resistivity** (кажущееся сопротивление), shown as a laterally deflecting curve
  - **SP (ПС) — Spontaneous potential** (потенциал самополяризации), the smoother companion curve
- The curves show significant variability in the upper portion of the log (more deflection/anomalies) transitioning to lower-amplitude, more regular response at depth — consistent with a transition from heterogeneous sedimentary cover into more homogeneous basement rocks
- Several **sharp anomalies or deflection peaks** are visible at specific depth intervals, potentially indicating permeable/porous intervals, lithological boundaries, or mineralised zones
- A notable **flat/quiet zone** appears in the lower-middle section, suggestive of massive, electrically uniform rock (possibly crystalline basement or evaporites)

**Right section:**
- A **tabular grid** occupying roughly the right third of the figure — this is characteristic of Soviet logs and typically contains:
  - Formation/interval descriptions (описание пород)
  - Core recovery percentages (выход керна, %)
  - Drilling parameters by interval
  - Possibly geochemical or lithological sample data
- The grid appears largely unfilled or lightly annotated in many intervals, which is common for intervals drilled without core recovery

**Header area:**
- Contains the well identification, coordinates, drilling organization, depth scale, and logging parameters
- A small inset box/legend appears in the upper right, likely giving scale factors for the curves and logging tool specifications

---

## Text and Labels

Due to the low resolution and faded/light printing quality of the image (characteristic of Soviet-era blueprint or ozalid/diazo print reproductions), precise transcription is limited. The following elements are discernible:

**Header (верхняя часть):**
- Title line likely reads something approximating: **"КАРОТАЖНАЯ ДИАГРАММА СКВАЖИНЫ №___"** (Wireline log of borehole No. ___)
- References to the survey organization and year (consistent with 1983–1984)
- Depth scale indicator
- Possible coordinates or map sheet reference

**Track labels (visible column headers):**
- Left margin depth scale in metres
- Curve identification labels above each track (КС, ПС or similar abbreviations)
- Stratigraphic column labels along left edge indicating geological periods/formations

**Right table headers:**
- Column headers for the tabular section, likely: **"Интервал"** (Interval), **"Мощность"** (Thickness), **"Описание"** (Description), **"Выход керна"** (Core recovery), etc.

**Lower portion:**
- The log continues below what appears to be a page break or fold line, with the lower section showing continued wireline curves — this may represent a **second run** or continuation to greater depth

**Small legend box (нижний правый угол):**
- Contains scale/calibration information for the logging curves

---

## Research Value

This borehole log provides the **primary subsurface lithological and petrophysical control** for the regional survey, allowing geologists to directly calibrate surface geophysical anomalies (gravity, magnetics, seismic) against actual rock properties encountered at depth. The resistivity and SP curves define **formation boundaries, fluid-bearing intervals, and potential mineralisation horizons** that cannot be resolved from surface surveys alone. For mineral potential assessment, the log's identification of basement depth, cover sequence thickness, and any anomalous resistivity zones (which could indicate sulphide mineralisation, hydrothermal alteration, or evaporite bodies) is essential for targeting further exploration in this part of northern Kazakhstan.
